claim_chain: Wide geographic variation exists in IPV-induced population immunity
Many countries will have immunity below 50% by 2031
Variation is attributable to differences in coverage, schedule choice, and timing
Early schedules provide minimal improvement over single dose
The 14-39 week schedule is optimal in all countries
Schedule changes and catch-up campaigns could substantially improve immunity
Risk assessments must consider country schedules when assessing IPV mitigation

typical_pattern: State current problem or finding with quantitative evidence
Present specific country examples or case studies

likely_priorities: Completeness of immunity picture beyond IPV alone
Impact of recent campaigns and catch-ups on projections
likely_attacks: Ignoring immunity from maternal antibodies and natural infection
Lack of uncertainty estimates for population immunity
Assuming constant RI coverage from 2023 forward
Not considering impact of number of injections on coverage
reviewer_preference_profile.inference_basis: The limitations section explicitly states: 'IPV-induced immunity only provides part of the total immunity', 'We have not given uncertainty estimates for population immunity because many of the data points lack uncertainty estimates', 'We have assumed that RI coverage remains constant from 2023', 'we have not considered that the number of injections a child is to be given at each clinic visit may affect coverage', and 'being whole-country estimates, can they capture subnational heterogeneity, which may be an important driver of polio risk where pockets of very low coverage exist'. These self-identified limitations directly indicate what reviewers would likely scrutinize.

examples.quoted_phrase: IPV-induced immunity will remain low, projected to be < 50% by 2031 in 16 countries and territories
it is crucial to recognise that introducing a second dose without consideration of vaccination schedule is insufficient
Countries and territories introducing a second IPV dose on the early schedule see little improvement over a single dose
there are unlikely to be large fluctuations

phrase_evidence.phrase: There is wide geographic variation in IPV-induced population immunity against poliomyelitis
This is especially troubling given the persistent, multiple-continent circulation of type-two vaccine-derived poliovirus
Progress has been made in recent years
However, it is crucial to recognise that
leaving children needlessly vulnerable to paralysis
For countries and territories yet to implement the second dose, schedule choice is paramount
While switches are not straightforward, they are necessary
Precedence exists in the case of Nepal
ethical questions surround leaving the children of those who adhere rigorously to the schedule less likely to receive protection
These high coverages obscure a troubling immunity landscape
While transitioning all countries and territories to a two-dose schedule at 14–39 weeks would reduce this to
Catch-up campaigns could accelerate improvement
periodic targeted IPV catch-up campaigns in select locations could yield substantial benefit
high protection rates against symptomatic infection in the under-five population are vital
risk assessments must consider country schedules and coverage when assessing risk mitigation from IPV
This is inadvisable, as it may be several years before Hexavalent introduction
This work has several limitations
IPV-induced immunity only provides part of the total immunity
Our estimates ignore immunity from maternal antibodies
We have not given uncertainty estimates for population immunity because many of the data points lack uncertainty estimates
We have assumed that RI coverage remains constant from 2023
there will be some exceptions where populations experiencing conflict
we have not considered that the number of injections a child is to be given at each clinic visit may affect coverage
Future research should address this
They are more reliable than administrative data but are not a perfect data source
nor, being whole-country estimates, can they capture subnational heterogeneity
estimated median serotype 2 population immunity in 2025 among children under five years of age is at 61%
The later 14–39 week schedule was optimal in all countries
IPV schedules and coverage are suboptimal in many countries
Those with a single dose should introduce a second on the 14–39 week schedule

verification_status.notes: All extracted phrases are literal substrings from the input text. All numeric values appear in the input. No external references, author names, or invented data were included. The profile is based solely on the discussion, abstract, and figure legend sections provided.
